title: Measuring with Meters
learningObjectives: Place measurement tools alongside a working circuit
Verify circuit validity before trusting readings
Relate measured voltage and current back to Ohm's Law
introText: Meters help you verify your math. You'll build a clean series loop, then add meters so you can compare readings to the calculated values.

steps.instructionText: Add a 9V source and a 330Ω resistor, then wire the circuit closed.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Circuit is complete. You're ready to measure it.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Wire the source and resistor together with no open ends. Every component needs both terminals connected.

steps.instructionText: Confirm the current is about 27 mA (I ≈ 9V / 330Ω).
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Reading is close to 27 mA. Your measurement matches the calculation.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Make sure the resistor is near 330Ω and the source near 9V. Current should be above 25 mA.

steps.instructionText: Place an ammeter and voltmeter in the workspace and wire them into the loop so you can visualize the readings.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Meters are in place and the supply is still about 9V. Measurements line up with your calculations.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Keep the source near 9V and be sure the meters are wired into the same loop. Unwired parts break the circuit.

questions.questionText: What should you check before trusting meter readings?
options: That the circuit is wired as a closed loop
That all components are the cheapest available
That the circuit has no resistors
questions.explanation: Meters only make sense on a valid circuit. Open connections give meaningless readings.

questions.questionText: With 9V across 330Ω, which reading do you expect?
